sentences.text: A lot.
See, everyone had a sheet with coupons.
When they came in the camp, they had to give that, yeah, hand that in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by handing that in, they were one of the inhabitants of the camp.
My husband had to take all their sheets in and wrote them, I suppose, on sheets.
And they went to head office.
Head office was at the time, I think, is [INAUDIBLE].
Instead of those sheets then, they had the right to get their share in the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So first they had to hand in those sheets.
They had to book them in.
And then later on, from every department, a hospital told them exactly how many there were in the hospital.
All the barracks told him how many inhabitants they had in the barracks.
And according to all those people, the coupons were-- were written out.
That was all done by his-- the men-- people who worked for him.
And in the beginning, there were 30.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't think they were all needed.
But that didn't matter, there were 30 people you could keep at that moment.
And that was our aim, to keep as many people into Poland as possible and not to give the Germans the chance to take them out.
But, later on, he wasn't allowed to have 30 anymore.
And he got 25.
And he got 20.
And so he only could keep a few at last, because we had I think 300 after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September '44.
And then it grew again a bit.
People who had been hiding and were found out and came to the campus.
S cases, [? strav ?] cases.
The--
Well, they were hiding, so they had been against the Germans, and they were put in separate barracks.
They were not allowed to walk free through the camp.
They, I think, we're not allowed to have [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] visitors from outside the-- of other people from the camp.
They were punished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Mainly keeping contact between people in the camp and outside.
If they needed something from outside, I went on my bike to Assen.
And from there on, they try to get papers in, what they needed or give a message through, what was needed.
What they called career jobs.
I went to Assen.
It was about 14 kilometers from the camp.
And I always did that on my bike.
Hmm, the back wheel was all right, but front wheel had a rubber strip.
Very hard, so I had several falls with it.
Because when there's a pebble in front of it and the wheel touches it, It turns over and you fall.
I don't know how I made it.
Yes, I was pregnant, yeah.
That was going to be our peace child.
He was born in July '45.
So, when the war ended, a lot of people came to me, kiss me, and said, now, we are going to have a child of peace, which was my son.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Bring messages over from outside to another.
Yeah, it all depended what was needed at that moment.
Sometimes it could be something of sending close.
Or it could be papers were to be editing or papers what was-- were needed to get out of the camp.
That was possible too.
Several ways.
Could be for to see a specialist, [INAUDIBLE].
They could get, yeah, a little note saying they were allowed to go out of the camp for that day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the underground movement, we had several of those stamped posts, of those stamps.
And we made false [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] notes saying it was done by the camp commander and stamped by the camp commander.
He never knew anything about it, but that was a chance to get them out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I had to go to Assen, and they made these papers ready there, and then I later picked them up and brought them in, so they could send them out and the people could get out.
Where would they go once they got out?
Somewhere.
Lots of people fled the camp that way.
We had it once-- and it was silly-- he went to relatives.
So when they started looking for him they picked him up.
And he came back, and then he was an S case, [? strav ?] case.
He never mentioned our names, luckily enough, but he was silly.
You never go back to your relative.
You never go back to a-- a known address.
He did.
Some were caught straight away, like he was.
Some of them left and never came back, and they survived the war outside the camp, being hidden somewhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But a lot of them were picked up again because there were always people who were willing to hide them, and often for a high price.
And when they got then scared that they would be found out, they mention their names.
And mind you, in that time, I think they could get something lower than a guilder for [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] telling the Germans where they could find the Jews.
They got so many per person.
And that was done, too.
Don't think they were all nice, oh, no.
Not all.
But, yeah, people were asking high amounts to hide them.
No, not in that time.
No.
Luckily enough, otherwise, I wouldn't be here.
You never-- you knew one and he introduced you to another one.
And the groups were really very small.
We had 20 all about.
And you always had to be careful.
For instance, we would never talk inside the house.
You had a saying in that times.
The walls have ears.
So my husband would say, in the middle of winter, let's go in the garden, have a look.
And then he would say, you have to go straight away to Assen.
You have to do so and so.
And then, in the afternoon, I went to Assen.
But never inside the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I don't think we were ever bugged.
You had-- didn't have that in the time.
But we went outside.
We never said it inside.
You must-- were you afraid?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In the beginning, you are afraid.
But a strange thing happens.
When you succeed, and next time you do a bit more dangerous and that goes well, you're not all that afraid anymore.
You know what to do now.
So we have done such silly things.
Later on, we said, for heaven's sake, how did we ever do it?
But everything went right, so you-- you did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, so many things.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Questions what were asked in the camp, making phone calls for people, it was always between people in the camp and people outside.
You never mentioned your name.
By ringing, you would say, well, so and so asked me to give you the message, so and so.
But you never mentioned your own name.
So people outside didn't know who rang them or--
Oh, they looked after it.
Oh, it was for the escape papers or the articles they needed.
Or, for instance, kids who were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in hiding, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the parents who were in the camp wanted to know how the child went.
So you rang someone and you said, now, so and so.
For instance, I had one, David in [INAUDIBLE] worrying about, what, [?
Yopi, ?] about [?
Yopi. ?] And they would get a little card and signed under, by [?
Yopi. ?] That things.
It was always between people inside and outside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Normal housewife.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes, and then second one came, and-- and I went around with the pram and walked through the camp.
And lot of people were-- knew me and--
By the Jews, marvelously.
That's why we made a lot of friends.
The Germans, I didn't see.
I ignored them.
Oh, yeah.
Problems, they had their own people working for them.
My husband wasn't one of them.
So if they had something specially going on, the family, even us, was never included.
If they could get something extra, a raincoat, or pajamas, or bonbons, or-- it was always a note.
Accept [INAUDIBLE] from us.
So good, they know that we are not one of them.
So--
Yeah, I had never heard a concert.
They had gotten a very good orchestra.
And they had to play for the Germans, to amuse the Germans.
In December, they had the cabaret and that was fantastic.
So I went to one cabaret.
I don't even know how we came there, but I went to one.
And that were-- were top artists, the best you could find.
So I applauded very enthusiastically because it was marvelous.
Next day, Mrs.
Van As was never allowed to come in to that hall anymore for whatever.
So I didn't hear one concert, and I didn't go to one other cabaret anymore.
Why were you forbidden?
Well, we were living there--
Well, because I had applauded.
You don't applaud for Jewish people.
I did.
Quite some time.
I think most of them left in September, '44.
No, the Commander liked cabarets, and he liked to be amused.
And he sat there every evening and-- but, at a certain time, they were all gone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September '44, they were nearly all gone, even the German Jews, and they were kept till the last.
Well, because German Jews were their first, they had started to build up the camp and to form, say, a committee.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were the [INAUDIBLE].
They had to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, they were the boss of the separate departments.
And they took care that their people were always safe.
And if someone had to go on transport, were Dutch Jews, never the German Jews.
They kept them as long as possible.
And they were called in the camps the [SPEAKING DUTCH], the-- the old camp inhabitants.
There was a bit of quarrel between old and-- and new, German and Dutch.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The-- the Germans had little, say, houses to live in.
The Dutch not.
They went into big, big barracks.
I think there were 600 or so in one barrack while the-- the-- the Germans had little-- little quarters were they could live with their own family.
Oh, don't ask me.
Large.
And the men were on that side, and the women were on that side.
And there was a little-- well, you could say kitchen in between.
I don't think they could cook there anything or so.
From the kitchen.
The kitchen was very big, and a lot of people were working there.
And they cooked potatoes and vegetables.
And we had a farm there.
They had their own potatoes.
And there came in a big truck every week with vegetables and fruit.
Not fruit for the Jews, vegetables for cooking.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we could get some vegetables and fruit from the men who brought it in.
Where was?
Oh, not that from the--
Yeah, Westerbork.
You've got a little township, Westerbork, but that's not where the camp was.
The camp was further up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You went for eight kilometers to Westerbork Town, township.
It wasn't a really town.
It was small.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I think about in 200 meters, perhaps, from the camp, was the farm.
And they grew potatoes and had pigs and cows.
I got my milk from the farm.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They looked after the farm.
And a lot of young folk were working there and singing while they worked on the fields.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah.
That woman came in in a wheelchair.
Hadn't walked, I don't exactly for how long, but for a very long time.
And when she came in the camp, she was put in a hospital.
And the hospital was marvelous.
So they looked very well after her, and they got her to walk again and be healthy.
And when she was better and she was healthy, she was sent on transport.
Well, that gave the people inside the camp the idea, see, they try to get to us all healthy.
And when we are healthy, they send us out to Poland.
Well, when the woman came there, one or two days later, she was gassed.
But no one was-- knew about it in that time.
We tried everything possible to-- to know what really happened in Poland.
For instance, my husband had a girl working for him, Leah Hertha-- Hertha Leah Alexander.
And she often came to my place.
And when she went on transport with her brother, I said, Hertha do me a pleasure.
Write a little card when you are in the camp, and when you really can't talk about it, instead of Hertha Leah write Leah Hertha.
Then I know I have to read exactly the opposite from what you are writing.
She said good idea.
I promise I'll do it.
We never got a card.
They were never allowed to send a card.
She survived, but she never had a chance to send anything.
And we tried-- people to put paper in the-- in the train.
And we had places in the train try to hide it there, that we can find it, how it is.
Now, the only thing what they ever wrote was it seems to be a town with lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] factories, because I see large pipes.
That was the only thing what we ever got to know-- know.
Later on, you learned that the pipes run from the gas ovens.
But whatever we tried during the war, we could never get to know what really happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Because we knew how it was in Westerbork.
We didn't know how it was in Poland.
So to be on the safe side, keep them here and try to work against the Germans as much as possible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For instance, a funny incident-- at a certain time, everybody in Holland had to hand in all their copper articles.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, you don't do what the Germans ask, so we hid all our copper articles under, in the cellar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father had to do it.
And he said, I'll teach them a lesson.
So he took a bag where coal had in been, with a lot of coal dust.
And he looked through his nails and he found some copper nails and he put them in the coal dust.
And then he went to the office to hand them in.
And the [?
Jep ?] said is that all you have got.
Well, isn't it enough, my father said and he turned to the [INAUDIBLE] and he was as black as-- little things you try to work against them, not to give them the way they wanted it.
Not involved, no.
They never knew what we did either.
For instance, my father and mother came to us, then we were living outside a camp.
They were not allowed to get into.
We were just living on the outside of the camp and it was house eight.
There were-- the Commander's house, the next one and another one.
And then six in a row, and we had the last one of it, closest to the farm.
Now, my parents came to visit us in-- on the 28th of August, '44.
And the beginning of September, the train was on strike.
No one worked anymore.
So they couldn't go back, and they stayed with us till the end of the war.
No, no.
We went outside in the garden.
And I would say, well, I'm going away this afternoon.
There may be a-- a-- a possibility I can get meat.
It was hard to.
So I went out to Assen, did my shopping, and sometimes I came back with meat.
Sometimes I said, well, it was all for nothing.
I couldn't get any.
Well, if I had to bring a message over, close by the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] killed people too, after the-- at the back of the crematorium, they killed people.
And my husband saw it, and he said go straight away to Assen.
Tell them, I think that first one was 15, had been killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went over to Assen, so they knew which group it was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Underground people too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, you could only give the message through.
Tell them what happened.
It was always dangerous.
You never knew when it was your time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Once, I think it was just after Nelly was born, I went to the office and I said to my husband, we should get out.
It's too dangerous now with the little ones, and we should get out.
And there was one in his office, Mr.
Veiner, ?] and he said, Mrs.
Van As, your husband's the only one who can help us.
Please, for our sake, stay here.
So we stayed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He survived.
He went to Theresienstadt.
[PAUSES FOR 67 SECONDS]
